$comment: Word-list provenance registry. SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for the license gate (scripts/license-gate.mjs) and the attribution surface (scripts/gen-credits.mjs -> credits.json). D2: exactly ONE source per language, no merging. A language MUST have a complete entry here AND a complete sources/<lang>/ directory (fetch script + LICENSE + PROVENANCE.md) before any wordlists/<lang>.txt is allowed to exist.

tiers.A: Ship-free. Permissive / public-domain (MIT, BSD, Apache-2.0, CC0, Unicode). No attribution required to redistribute derived data.
tiers.B: Attribution + share-alike. Copyleft or CC BY-SA (GPL, LGPL, MPL, CC BY-SA). Must credit the source (credits.json) and preserve the source's terms on the DATA files. NOTE: shipping copyleft-derived data inside a closed binary is a posture decision for Eric, not this pipeline.
tiers.C: Restricted. Usable only unmodified and/or server-side, or otherwise limited. STOP AND ASK before any use; never ship a modified/derived list.

es.name: Recursos Lingüísticos Abiertos del Español (rla-es)

credits.name: Recursos Lingüísticos Abiertos del Español (rla-es)
credits.attribution: Spanish word data derived from Recursos Lingüísticos Abiertos del Español (rla-es), © the RLA-ES contributors, tri-licensed GPLv3 / LGPLv3 / MPL-1.1.

es.$verdict_note: Tier B (GPL-3.0-or-later OR LGPL-3.0-or-later OR MPL-1.1) suggests PERMITTED_WITH_ATTRIBUTION, and credits.json already carries the attribution — but a tier is a shelving decision, not a legal verdict, and deriving permitted_use from it is exactly the cleverness the addendum forbids. Also unresolved: this file's own note that shipping copyleft-derived data inside a CLOSED App Store binary is a posture decision for Eric. That question gates this verdict.

de.name: Original curation (SpellGame)
de.license: Owned — original work authored for this app
de.$verdict_note: assets/words/LICENSES.md records these banks as 'Original curation for this app — Owned — ships freely — Hand-authored common vocabulary'. If that holds, the verdict is PERMITTED and Eric can promote it in one edit. It is UNKNOWN here only because no human has said so IN THE MANIFEST, which is the whole point of a machine-checked claim: the proposal sentence is 'every word list traces to a documented source whose license permits this use, and the build fails otherwise', and prose in a README is not that.

$licenseAddendum: CC-WORDLIST-SOURCES license addendum (2026-07-16), ADAPTED. The addendum specifies one generated licenses.json with per-source permitted_use / verified_by / verified_date / content_hash. This repo already had a per-language source-of-record (this file) + provenance validation + credits.json, and the addendum says to use an existing integrity mechanism if one exists. So the verdict fields live HERE, on each source, and the integrity binding is the EXISTING pair — tarball_sha256 binds the SOURCE, and wordlists/<lang>.provenance.json binds every shipped curated word to that source's surface index. That is strictly stronger than hashing the shipped list: a content_hash tells you the list CHANGED, provenance tells you every word in it is still backed. Deviation flagged for Eric; no separate licenses.json is generated. `node scripts/emit-license-table.mjs` renders this file for proposal appendices.

permitted_use_values.PERMITTED_WITH_ATTRIBUTION: Ship with the credit in credits.json. Tier B.
permitted_use_values.PROHIBITED: Must not back a shipped list.
permitted_use_values.UNKNOWN: No human verdict yet. FAILS the gate for any ACTIVE language; warns for one still gated (audit/RTL), mirroring the activation-gate philosophy: enforcement tightens where content becomes reachable.

verification: verified_by MUST name a person — the addendum is explicit that only a human edit promotes a verdict, and that the generator must never infer a license from a URL, a filename, or a tier. Entries below are PRE-FILLED as UNKNOWN, which is the generator doing its job, not an omission. Eric (or a named auditor) promotes them by editing verified_by + verified_date.
